summary: DHH reflects on the trajectory of 'no politics at work' policies, noting that when 37signals adopted this stance in 2021, it caused massive public backlash and employee departures. Now, less than two years later, Meta has implemented virtually the same policy for its 70,000+ employees and the response has been near-silence. He argues this proves the cultural shift he predicted is well underway, with even former critics privately seeking advice on implementing similar policies. DHH frames this as genuine progress and connects it to what he sees as the declining influence of DEI initiatives in corporate culture.
key_points: 37signals' no-politics policy in 2021 caused weeks of drama and ~20 employee departures, but DHH considers it one of the most valuable decisions for long-term company health
Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong reports similar validation, with even vocal critics privately reaching out for advice on implementing the same approach
Meta quietly banned internal discussion of abortion, guns, vaccines, and elections with virtually zero media or public backlash
The contrast between the 60-person company causing a firestorm and the 70,000-person company causing silence illustrates how quickly the Overton window shifted
DHH connects this trend to the broader decline of DEI's corporate influence and predicts more companies will follow
money_quotes: Being early can be costly. But the results, despite the (very) difficult transition, have ultimately been worth it.
No comment thread on Basecamp is going to close the gap on fundamental philosophical and political differences. And we're left worse for wear when we try.

That's what real progress looks like. That we in less than two years can go from no-politics etiquettes being major industry news, when a company of sixty people does it, to being a complete nothingburger, when a company of 70,000+ does it.

Most people are simply ready to move on from this nonsense. They don't actually want to discuss abortion, gun rights, vaccine efficacy, or elections with their coworkers during work hours in work forums.

You'd think that such an important shift for a company that still has more than 70,000 employees would be A Very Big Deal to all the journalists, activists, and internet commentators who lost their shit when Coinbase and 37signals first took that position. But you'd be wrong. It's been crickets.
After living with this type of etiquette for some time now, I would not want to go back.
key_insight: The near-silent reception of Meta's no-politics-at-work policy proves that what was once a career-risking corporate stance has become mainstream common sense in under two years.
